21.correct: The Governor of a state sends a report to the President recommending President's Rule (Article 356) when the state government cannot function according to the Constitution.

wrong.A: The Chief Minister heads the state government that is being superseded; he does not recommend President's Rule.
wrong.C: The Prime Minister advises on national matters but does not formally recommend state emergency.
wrong.D: The Supreme Court can review the proclamation but does not recommend its imposition.

21.fact: President's Rule under Article 356 must be approved by Parliament within two months and the S.R. Bommai (1994) judgment made it subject to judicial review.

22.correct: When no party has a clear majority in the state assembly, the Governor uses discretion to appoint as Chief Minister the person most likely to command the confidence of the House.

wrong.A: The President is concerned with the Union government, not appointing a state CM.
wrong.B: The Speaker of the Assembly presides over the House but does not appoint the CM.
wrong.C: The Prime Minister has no role in appointing a state Chief Minister.

22.fact: The Governor's discretion here is one of the few genuine discretionary powers under the Constitution, exercised especially in hung assemblies.

23.correct: To study the history of Indian music, one must first understand human origin and the basic elements of sound - swara (tone), and laya (rhythm) - from which all music developed.

wrong.A: Human civilisation and language are background context, but music specifically rests on sound, tone and rhythm.
wrong.B: Human evolution and technical skill are too general and not the core musical elements.
wrong.D: The development of instruments and dance came after the fundamental elements of sound.

23.fact: Indian classical theory is built on the concepts of swara (notes), raga (melodic framework) and tala (rhythmic cycle).

24.correct: By Newton's law of gravitation, force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance. If the distance increases 6 times, the force becomes 1/6^2 = 1/36, i.e. 36 times smaller.

wrong.A: Force decreases, not increases, when distance grows, so '36 times larger' is wrong.
wrong.B: Force does not increase 6 times; it decreases with the square of distance.
wrong.D: Force falls by the square (1/36), not just 1/6.

24.fact: Newton's law: F = G m1 m2 / r^2, the inverse-square law of gravitation.

25.correct: 'Memories of Midnight' (1990) is Sidney Sheldon's sequel to his earlier bestseller 'The Other Side of Midnight'.

wrong.B: 'Master of the Game' is a separate Sidney Sheldon novel, not a sequel to The Other Side of Midnight.
wrong.C: 'Morning, Noon and Night' is another standalone Sheldon novel.
wrong.D: 'Bloodline' is a different Sidney Sheldon thriller, not the sequel.

25.fact: Both novels follow the character Catherine Alexander and the schemes of Constantin Demiris.

26.correct: The Malwa Plateau lies mainly in Madhya Pradesh (extending into parts of Rajasthan and Gujarat) and is a volcanic, black-soil plateau in west-central India.

wrong.B: Maharashtra is associated with the Deccan Plateau, not the Malwa Plateau.
wrong.C: Only the north-western fringe of Malwa touches Rajasthan; the plateau is principally in MP.
wrong.D: Gujarat lies to the west and holds only a small edge of the region.

26.fact: The Malwa Plateau is drained by rivers like the Chambal and Betwa and is known for its fertile black soil.

27.correct: The Bhabar is a narrow belt of porous, pebbly soil at the foothills of the Himalayas, where rivers descending from the mountains deposit coarse sediments and streams often disappear underground.

wrong.B: The Peninsular Plateau is an ancient landmass of the south, unrelated to the Bhabar.
wrong.C: The Indo-Gangetic plain lies south of the Bhabar; the Terai (not Bhabar) adjoins it.
wrong.D: The Bhabar is a humid foothill zone, not a desert region.

27.fact: Just south of the Bhabar lies the marshy Terai belt, where the underground streams re-emerge.

28.correct: The Global Liveability Index is published by the Economist Intelligence Unit (EIU), ranking cities on stability, healthcare, culture, environment, education and infrastructure.

wrong.B: The World Bank publishes development indicators and the Ease of Doing Business work, not this index.
wrong.C: The UNDP publishes the Human Development Index, not the Liveability Index.
wrong.D: The World Economic Forum publishes the Global Competitiveness Report, not this one.

28.fact: Vienna has frequently topped the EIU's Global Liveability Index in recent years.

29.correct: Archery returned to the Olympic programme in 1972, the year associated with the international governing body's formal recognition for the sport at the Olympics.

wrong.A: 1932 is too early; archery was absent from the Olympics for decades after 1920.
wrong.D: 1992 is far later than the recognition year.

29.fact: World Archery (formerly FITA) was founded in 1931 and governs the sport internationally.

30.correct: NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) is a joint Earth-observation satellite that uses radar to monitor changes in the Earth's surface in all weather, day or night, even through clouds.

wrong.A: Chandrayaan-3 is India's lunar lander-rover mission, not an Earth-surface radar satellite.
wrong.B: EOS-09 is an Earth-observation satellite but is not the NASA-ISRO radar collaboration described.
wrong.D: XPoSat is an X-ray polarimetry satellite for studying cosmic sources, not Earth's surface.

30.fact: NISAR carries dual-frequency (L- and S-band) radar, the first satellite of its kind, to track land deformation, ice and ecosystems.

31.correct: The GSLV Mk III (now called LVM3) is nicknamed the 'Fat Boy' because of its heavy, bulky build; it is ISRO's most powerful launch vehicle.

wrong.A: The PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is ISRO's reliable workhorse, not the 'Fat Boy'.
wrong.C: The SSLV is the Small Satellite Launch Vehicle, a lightweight rocket.
wrong.D: The ASLV was an early, much smaller experimental launch vehicle.

31.fact: LVM3 launched Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3 and will carry India's Gaganyaan crewed mission.

32.correct: Lt Cdr Yashasvi Solanki became the first woman naval officer to serve as an Aide-de-Camp (ADC) to the President of India.

wrong.B: Lt Bhavna Singh is not the officer associated with this first.
wrong.C: Commander Priya Menon is not the record-holder named here.
wrong.D: Captain Shikha Sharma is not the first woman naval ADC to the President.

32.fact: An Aide-de-Camp is a personal military assistant to a high official such as the President, who is the Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces.

33.correct: The Industrial Policy of 1977 (under the Janata government) gave special prominence to small-scale and cottage industries, expanding the list of items reserved for them and promoting decentralised development.

wrong.A: The 1948 policy laid the first framework for a mixed economy but did not specially emphasise small industries.
wrong.B: The 1956 policy emphasised heavy industry and the public sector ('commanding heights').
wrong.D: The 1980 policy focused on raising productivity and reviving the public sector, not chiefly on small industry.

33.fact: The 1977 policy introduced the concept of 'District Industries Centres' to promote small enterprises.

34.correct: India's first oil refinery was established at Digboi in Assam (commissioned in 1901), making it one of the oldest operating refineries in the world.

wrong.A: Haldia in West Bengal has a much later refinery.
wrong.B: Koyali (Gujarat) refinery came up in the 1960s.
wrong.C: Barauni refinery in Bihar was set up in the 1960s.

34.fact: Digboi is often called the 'Oil City of Asia'; oil was first struck there in the 1880s.

35.correct: If nominal GDP rises faster than real GDP, the gap is due to rising prices - this indicates high inflation, because nominal GDP is measured at current prices while real GDP is adjusted for price changes.

wrong.A: Zero growth would mean GDP is not changing, which is not the case here.
wrong.B: Falling inflation would narrow, not widen, the gap between nominal and real GDP.
wrong.D: Deflation means falling prices, which would make nominal GDP grow slower than real GDP.

35.fact: The ratio of nominal to real GDP is the GDP deflator, a broad measure of economy-wide inflation.

36.correct: The main weakness of India's balance of trade is a persistent trade deficit - the value of imports (especially crude oil, gold and electronics) consistently exceeds the value of exports.

wrong.A: A surplus in services exports is actually a strength that partly offsets the goods deficit, not a weakness.
wrong.B: Over-dependence on a few partners is a concern but not the defining weakness of the trade balance.
wrong.D: A high tariff structure is a policy feature, not the core trade-balance problem.

36.fact: India's large software and services exports help finance its merchandise (goods) trade deficit in the current account.

37.correct: The PM-DAKSH (Pradhan Mantri Dakshta Aur Kushalta Sampann Hitgrahi) Yojana, a skilling scheme for marginalised groups, was approved with an outlay of about Rs 450 crore for 2021-22 to 2025-26.

wrong.A: The SMILE scheme supports the welfare and rehabilitation of transgender persons and beggars, not this skilling outlay.
wrong.C: The NAMASTE scheme is for the safety and dignity of sanitation workers.
wrong.D: Nasha Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan is a drug-free India campaign, not a skilling scheme.

37.fact: PM-DAKSH targets SCs, OBCs, EWS, safai karamcharis and DNTs for free skill training, implemented by the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment.

38.correct: The kingdom of Mysore controlled the lucrative trade of the Malabar coast (south-western India), an important source of pepper and spices, which brought it into conflict with the English.

wrong.A: Bombay (the Konkan coast) was a Maratha/English sphere, not controlled by Mysore.
wrong.B: Hooghly is on the eastern (Bengal) coast, far from Mysore's influence.
wrong.C: The Coromandel coast (south-east) was dominated by the British and French at Madras/Pondicherry.

38.fact: Mysore's control of Malabar's pepper and sandalwood trade under Haidar Ali and Tipu Sultan was a key cause of the Anglo-Mysore Wars.

39.correct: After the Revolt of 1857, the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar was tried and exiled (imprisoned) to Rangoon in Burma, where he died in 1862.

wrong.A: Shanghai (China) was never a place of his imprisonment.
wrong.B: Dhaka was not where he was sent.
wrong.D: The Andaman Islands (Cellular Jail) held many later freedom fighters, but Zafar was exiled to Rangoon.

39.fact: Bahadur Shah Zafar was also a noted Urdu poet; his tomb in Yangon (Rangoon) is a site of pilgrimage.

40.correct: Atal Bihari Vajpayee first became Prime Minister of India in 1996, heading a government that lasted only 13 days before resigning.

wrong.A: In 1990 V.P. Singh / Chandra Shekhar were in office, not Vajpayee.
wrong.B: 1992 was during P.V. Narasimha Rao's tenure.
wrong.C: 1994 was also during Narasimha Rao's term.

40.fact: Vajpayee served three terms as PM (13 days in 1996, 13 months in 1998-99, and a full term 1999-2004).
